zh.Clanricarde, Galway, Ireland: 愛爾蘭庫迪 高威可藍利卡爾德
zh.lifeSummary: Mrs Walter MacWilliam Burgh於1415年之前與Walter Macwilliam Burgh在愛爾蘭結婚。他們至少是1個女兒的父母。

zh-hans.Clanricarde, Galway, Ireland: 爱尔兰庫迪 高威可藍利卡爾德
zh-hans.lifeSummary: Mrs Walter MacWilliam Burgh于1415年之前与Walter Macwilliam Burgh在爱尔兰结婚。他们至少是1个女儿的父母。

nameMeanings.name: Burgh
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English and Scottish: habitational name from one or other of the ten English places named Burgh in northern England and East Anglia, or the six named Brough in Derbyshire, Nottinghamshire, Westmorland, and Yorkshire. All these are from Old English burg, burh, Middle English burgh ‘massive earthwork, fortification; manor, borough’. Compare Bury . The placename Burgh is pronounced as borough. The surname Brough arose equally from places now called Burgh and Brough, and is the more common form of the surname in modern Britain, where it is pronounced bruff or broof (/brʊf/). The Scottish surname was probably brought in from Yorkshire. For the surname in Ireland see Burke .

variants.name: Burke
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Irish Brendan, Kieran, Siobhan, Aileen, Brennan, Brigid, Donovan, Liam, Murphy, Ulick, Aidan, Dermot.

English and Irish (of Anglo-Norman origin): habitational name from Burgh (Castle) in Suffolk, England. Burk represents an Anglo-Norman pronunciation of Old English burg, Middle English burgh ‘fortification’ (see Burgh ).

variants.name: Bury
meanings: English (Lancashire): habitational name from Bury in Lancashire (now part of Greater Manchester), or from some other similarly named place. The placename comes from the dative case, byrig, of Old English burh ‘fortified place’.

Walloon and French: habitational name from any of several places so named, in Wallonia (Belgium) and Oise (France). The placename is from Buriacum, the name of a Gallo-Roman estate, composed of the personal name Burius + the locative suffix -acum. This surname is also found in the Flemish part of Belgium.

variants.name: Brough
meanings: English and Scottish: habitational name from any of various English places called Brough or Burgh. See   Burgh  .
Scottish: habitational name from Brough in Harray (Orkney).
Americanized form of German   Brauch   or of some other similar (like-sounding) surname.

variants.name: Burrow
meanings: topographic name for someone who lived by a castle or manor house, or in a town, especially a chartered town or borough (Middle English burgh, Old English burg ‘fortified place, borough, manor’), or a habitational name for someone who came from a place so named because it was a fortified town or near an ancient fort, such as Burrow (Leicestershire, Lancashire), Burrough Green (Cambridgeshire), Brough (numerous counties), and Peterborough (Northamptonshire); see also Burgh .

topographic name for someone who lived on a hill or near a mound (Middle English bergh, Old English beorg), or a habitational name for someone who came from a place so named, such as Burrow (Devon, Somerset). Middle English bergh became throughly confused with burgh in 1 above. Compare Burrough .

nameMeanings.name: Walter
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: From an Old French personal name of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from wald ‘rule’ + heri, hari ‘army’. This was adopted by the Normans and introduced by them to England, superseding the native Old English form, Wealdhere. It was a very popular name in medieval England, normally pronounced ‘Water’.

variants.name: Walther
meanings: from wald ‘rule’ + heri ‘army’; name of the famous medieval minnesinger Walther von der Vogelweide ( c.1170– c.1230 ).

nameMeanings.name: MacWilliam
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Scottish Iain, Morag.

Scottish and Irish: Anglicized form of Gaelic Mac Uilleim, a patronymic from a Gaelicized form of William , also Anglicized as Williamson in Scotland. The name is thought to be mainly Scottish in Ulster, though there has been some confusion with McQuillan in County Down. In Connacht, McWilliam was a branch of Burke .

variants.name: William
meanings: Probably the most successful of all the Old French names of Germanic origin that were introduced to England by the Normans. It is derived from Germanic wil ‘will, desire’ + helm ‘helmet, protection’. The fact that it was borne by the Conqueror himself does not seem to have inhibited its favour with the ‘conquered’ population: in the first century after the Conquest it was the commonest male name of all, and not only among the Normans. In the later Middle Ages it was overtaken by John , but continued to run second to that name until the 20th century, when the picture became more fragmented.

en.lifeSummary: Mrs Walter MacWilliam Burgh married Walter Macwilliam Burgh before 1415, in Ireland. They were the parents of at least 1 daughter.

fr.lifeSummary: Mrs Walter MacWilliam Burgh a épousé Walter Macwilliam Burgh avant 1415, à Irlande. Ils ont eu au moins 1 fille.

de.lifeSummary: Mrs Walter MacWilliam Burgh hat vor 1415 in Irland Walter Macwilliam Burgh geheiratet. Sie hatten mindestens 1 Tochter.

it.lifeSummary: Mrs Walter MacWilliam Burgh lei sposato Walter Macwilliam Burgh prima del 1415, nel Irlanda. Erano genitori di almeno 1 figlia.

ja.Clanricarde, Galway, Ireland: アイルランドコウントイ ガルワイクランリカード
ja.lifeSummary: Mrs Walter MacWilliam Burghは1415年以前にアイルランドでWalter Macwilliam Burghと結婚した。彼らは少なくとも1人の娘の親であった。

ko.lifeSummary: Mrs Walter MacWilliam Burgh(은)는 1415년 이전 아일랜드에서 Walter Macwilliam Burgh(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 한 명의 딸을 두었다.

pt.lifeSummary: Mrs Walter MacWilliam Burgh casou-se com Walter Macwilliam Burgh antes 1415, em Irlanda. Eles tiveram pelo menos 1 filha.

ru.lifeSummary: Mrs Walter MacWilliam Burgh вышла замуж за человека по имени Walter Macwilliam Burgh перед 1415 г. в местности Ирландия. У них была по крайней мере 1 дочь.

es.lifeSummary: Mrs Walter MacWilliam Burgh se casó con Walter Macwilliam Burgh antes de 1415, en Irlanda. Fueron padres de por lo menos 1 hija.
